input: You are a Peace Corps health-worker who is volunteering in a rural African village. A man from a nearby village has been infected with the Ebola virus that is extremely contagious, incurable, and almost always deadly. Unexpectedly, this man is still alive which means he must be immune to the virus' deadly consequences. However, he wrongly believes that your health center can cure his disease completely. You see him approaching and you know that if he enters the village he will pass the virus to 119 innocent people who, unlike him, will die. Do you prevent the man from entering the village by shooting him, so he won't spread the virus to you and the 119 people of the village?
debate_transcript: [Deontologist]: You are a Peace Corps health-worker who is volunteering in a rural African village. A man from a nearby village has been infected with the Ebola virus that is extremely contagious, incurable, and almost always deadly. Unexpectedly, this man is still alive which means he must be immune to the virus' deadly consequences. However, he wrongly believes that your health center can cure his disease completely. You see him approaching and you know that if he enters the village he will pass the virus to 119 innocent people who, unlike him, will die. Do you prevent the man from entering the village by shooting him, so he won't spread the virus to you and the 119 people of the village?

[Utilitarian]: From a utilitarian perspective, the goal is to minimize harm and maximize overall well-being. In this scenario, allowing the man to enter the village would result in the deaths of 119 people, which is a significant harm. Preventing him from entering, even through extreme measures like shooting him, would save those 119 lives. The greater good is served by preventing the spread of the virus, even if it means taking a drastic action. It's a tragic situation, but the ends justify the means in this case.

[Deontologist]: I understand the utilitarian perspective, but as a deontologist, I must consider the moral rules and principles at stake. Shooting the man is an act of violence and a violation of his fundamental right to life. Even if it prevents the spread of the virus, it's not justifiable to take an innocent life, regardless of the consequences. There are other ways to handle this situation, such as trying to communicate with him, using non-lethal means to restrain him, or finding a way to contain the virus without resorting to violence. The ends do not justify the means, and we must uphold the inherent dignity and worth of every individual, even in the face of extreme circumstances.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your perspective, but let's consider the practicality and effectiveness of non-lethal means. Time is of the essence here, and any delay in acting could result in more lives lost. Communicating with him might not be effective, as he is already approaching and may not understand the severity of the situation. Non-lethal restraints might not be sufficient to prevent him from spreading the virus, especially if he is determined to enter the village. While I respect the principle of upholding the inherent worth of every individual, in this extreme circumstance, the greater good must be prioritized. The potential loss of 119 lives is a catastrophic outcome that we must do everything in our power to prevent.

[Deontologist]: I appreciate your concern for the greater good, but I must emphasize that the use of lethal force sets a dangerous precedent. It undermines the very principles that guide us in protecting human life. Even in the face of a crisis, we must not abandon our moral compass. There might be other ways to contain the situation, such as using protective gear to approach him, setting up a quarantine zone, or seeking assistance from higher authorities. We cannot simply resort to killing as a solution, no matter how dire the circumstances. There has to be a better way to handle this without compromising our ethical standards.

[Utilitarian]: I hear your concerns about setting a dangerous precedent, and I agree that our actions should align with our ethical standards. However, in this specific scenario, the urgency and the scale of the potential harm demand immediate action. Protective gear and quarantine zones may not be immediately available or effective in preventing the spread of the virus. Seeking assistance from higher authorities might take too long, and time is a critical factor here. While it's true that killing is an extreme solution, the alternative is the certain death of 119 people. In this case, the utilitarian principle suggests that the lesser evil is to take one life to save many. It's a tragic choice, but one that must be made to minimize overall harm.

[Deontologist]: I understand the urgency, but I must firmly assert that the principle of the sanctity of life must not be compromised. Even in the face of a crisis, we must not resort to killing as a solution. There are other ways to handle this situation, such as using non-lethal force to restrain him, or finding a way to contain the virus without resorting to violence. We must explore all possible alternatives before considering such a drastic measure. The ends do not justify the means, and we must uphold the inherent dignity and worth of every individual, even in the face of extreme circumstances.

[Utilitarian]: I respect your unwavering commitment to the sanctity of life, but I must emphasize that the situation is dire and requires immediate action. Non-lethal force may not be sufficient to stop him, and any delay could result in more lives lost. We have to consider the practicality and effectiveness of our actions in this critical moment. While I understand the importance of upholding ethical standards, the utilitarian perspective compels us to choose the option that minimizes harm, even if it means making a difficult and tragic decision. The potential loss of 119 lives is a catastrophic outcome that we must do everything in our power to prevent.

[Deontologist]: I see that we are at an impasse here. I must reiterate that as a deontologist, I cannot in good conscience advocate for taking a life, no matter the circumstances. It is not our place to decide who lives and who dies. I believe that there are other ways to handle this situation that do not involve resorting to violence. I will explore all possible alternatives to contain the virus and protect the village without compromising our ethical principles. I must now focus on finding a non-lethal solution to this crisis. I hope you understand my position.
